name: Siftables
short_description: A platform of compact, sensing, display-equipped tiles that people manipulate as a physical group to interact with digital information.
long_description: Siftables, introduced by David Merrill, Jeevan Kalanithi, and Pattie Maes at TEI 2007, are small cookie-sized devices that each carry a graphical display, motion sensing (tilt, shake, rotation via an accelerometer), neighbor detection, and wireless communication. By physically arranging, tilting, and clustering the tiles, users manipulate collections of digital media and content directly with their hands, much like rearranging physical blocks. As an early embodiment of tangible and sensor-network user interfaces, it matters to physical AI and interaction design because it shows how distributed, manipulable objects can make abstract digital information graspable and spatially legible without screens, keyboards, or mice.
ai_note: Siftables is a tangible sensor-network interface; behavior comes from accelerometer/neighbor sensing and rule-based application logic, with no machine learning running in the system.

images.caption: Siftables tiles being manipulated as a group, from the TEI 2007 paper figures.
images.alt_text: A cluster of small square electronic tiles, each with a graphical display, arranged together on a surface and handled by a person.

sources.title: Siftables: towards sensor network user interfaces
sources.publisher: ACM (Proceedings of the 1st International Conference on Tangible and Embedded Interaction, TEI '07)
